elements.sent-2411-001: There is a long tradition \cite{shelly1911cuestion,barbeau1961remarks,ufnarovski2003differentiate, buium2005arithmetic,stay2005generalized,kovic2012arithmetic,buium2015differential,pasten2022arithmetic,buium2023foundations of attempting to differentiate numbers in various ways.}
elements.sent-2411-002: Much attention was focused on derivatives of numbers when Jeffries' paper on the subject appeared in the Notices of the AMS late last year \cite{jeffries1772differentiating ; almost simultaneously (and apparently independently), Tossavainen et al's survey on the subject appeared in the College Math Journal \cite{tossavainen2024we .}}
elements.sent-2411-003: Why should the reader care about differentiating numbers? In general, any time a new theory is introduced, it is natural to seek numerical structures satisfying that theory: thus, when the theory of groups is introduced, it is natural to introduce examples like $(\mathbb Q,+)$ and $(\mathbb R^+,\cdot)$.
elements.sent-2411-004: Theories about elementary calculus functions, in languages including the unary function symbol $\bullet'$, were originally modeled by structures whose universes consisted of elementary calculus functions, not numbers.
elements.sent-2411-005: We can at least try to find numerical models for these theories.
elements.sent-2411-006: The act of interpreting $\bullet'$ in a structure whose universe is a number system is the act of ``differentiating numbers''.
elements.sent-2411-007: We are hopeful that generalizing models of elementary calculus function theories could eventually be fruitful just like generalizing models of permutation sets led to the abstract theory of groups.
elements.sent-2411-008: When it comes to numerically modeling theories, different theories might require different number systems: both $(\mathbb Z,+,\cdot)$ and $(\mathbb Q,+,\cdot)$ are rings, but only the latter is a field.
elements.sent-2411-009: By gaining knowledge about which number systems are needed for which theories, we gain insight into those theories.
elements.sent-2411-010: We hope that a greater knowledge about which number systems are needed to model various subtheories of elementary calculus functions, will eventually give us insight into those subtheories.
elements.sent-2411-011: If the only axiom we care about is the Leibniz rule (and the nontriviality axiom $\exists x\,s.t.\,x'\not=0$), we can interpret $\bullet'$ on $\mathbb N$ so as to satisfy that.
elements.sent-2411-013: But their $(\mathbb N,\bullet')$ does not even satisfy the linearity axiom.
elements.sent-2411-014: Our interpretation (in Section \ref{mainsection ) of $\bullet'$ on a subset of the hyperreals will satisfy far more axioms of the theory of elementary calculus functions.}
elements.sent-2411-015: And in Section \ref{entirenumberssectn , we will introduce a stricter subset of the hyperreals where not only $\bullet'$ can be elegantly interpreted, but $\circ$ as well (in other words, there is an elegant way to define the ``composition'' of two numbers there), in such a way as to numerically satisfy even the chain rule.}
elements.sent-2411-016: The key idea behind our numerical interpretation of $\bullet'$ is to commute the derivative operation with the operation of taking a function's equivalence class in the hyperreals, in other words, define $[f]'=[f']$ (we will spell out the details below).
elements.sent-2411-017: Unfortunately, this is not well-defined in general.
elements.sent-2411-018: However, the idea can be salvaged in several different ways, by making use of certain idempotent ultrafilters.
elements.sent-2411-019: In Section \ref{finitecalculussection we will use the same approach to well-define a hyperreal variation of the derivative from finite calculus, and as an application of that, we will give a new proof of Hindman's theorem and also strengthen said theorem.}

elements.subsection-2411-001: Differential equations and the secant method
elements.sent-2411-090: Since $\bullet'$ takes (a subset of) ${ ^*\mathbb R$ to ${ ^*\mathbb R$, one can attempt to solve (or approximately solve) differential equations by using the secant method from numerical analysis, which is traditionally only used to solve non-differential equations.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: This is interesting because as far as we know, the secant method has not previously been applicable to differential equations.
elements.sent-2411-092: We illustrate this with an example in which the method finds a correct solution in one step.
elements.sent-2411-093: Solve the differential equation $y'-2x=0$ using the secant method, with initial guesses $y_0=x^2+x^3$ and $y_1=x^2-x^3$.
elements.sent-2411-094: Define $\alpha:{\subseteq { ^*\mathbb R\to{ ^*\mathbb R$ by $\alpha([f])=[f]'-2\Omega$ whenever $[f]'$ is defined.}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: We desire a solution $[f]$ of the equation $\alpha([f])=0$.
elements.sent-2411-096: Since $\Omega=[x\mapsto x]$, it follows that $[f]'-2\Omega=[x\mapsto f'(x)-2x]$, so any such solution $[f]$ will yield a solution $y=f(x)$ to the differential equation $y'-2x=0$ (at least $p$-a.e.).
elements.sent-2411-097: Compute: \begin{equation}[f_0] = \Omega^2+\Omega^3 (initial guess $y_0=x^2+x^3$) [f_1] = \Omega^2-\Omega^3 (initial guess $y_1=x^2-x^3$) [f_2] = [f_1] - \alpha([f_1]) \frac{[f_1]-[f_0]}{\alpha([f_1])-\alpha([f_0])}; (Secant method) \alpha([f_1]) = (\Omega^2-\Omega^3)'-2\Omega = (2\Omega-3\Omega^2)-2\Omega; \alpha([f_0]) = (\Omega^2+\Omega^3)'-2\Omega = (2\Omega+3\Omega^2)-2\Omega;\end{equation} it follows that $[f_2]=\Omega^2$.
elements.sent-2411-098: This yields a solution $y=x^2$ to the original differential equation.
elements.sent-2411-099: We hope that either such examples can be found later, or, if not, that the lack of such examples might provide insight into limitations of the secant method itself.
elements.sent-2411-100: The point of this subsection is not so much to focus on the secant method, but rather to illustrate the kind of things we hope might be possible by numerically interpreting the theory of elementary calculus functions.
elements.subsection-2411-002: The well-definability of composition on a subset of the hyperreals
elements.sent-2411-101: The work we have presented above is relevant to numerically modeling subtheories of the theory of elementary calculus functions in a language containing a unary function symbol $\bullet'$ for differentiation.
elements.sent-2411-102: But one key axiom is missing from that theory, namely the chain rule, since the chain rule also involves a binary function symbol $\circ$ for composition.
elements.sent-2411-103: In this section, we introduce a subset of the hyperreals suitable for numerically modeling subtheories of the theory of elementary calculus functions in a language containing $\bullet'$ and $\circ$.
elements.sent-2411-104: The following definition is motivated by the theory of complex analysis.
elements.sent-2411-105: A function $f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R$ is entire if $f$ is infinitely differentiable at $0$ and $\forall x\in\mathbb R$, $f(x)=\sum_{k=0 ^\infty f^{(k) (0)x^k/k!$.}}
elements.sent-2411-106: A hyperreal number is entire if it can be written as $[f]$ for some entire $f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R$.
elements.sent-2411-107: For all entire $f,g:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R$, if $[f]=[g]$ then $f=g$.
elements.sent-2411-108: By well-known results from analysis, $f$ and $g$ are infinitely differentiable everywhere, so $[f^{(k) ]$ and $[g^{(k) ]$ exist for all $k$.}}
elements.sent-2411-109: By repeated applications of Theorem \ref{firsttheorem , each $[f^{(k) ]=[g^{(k) ]$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: By Lemma \ref{neatlemma , it follows that each $f^{(k) (0)=g^{(k) (0)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: Since $f$ and $g$ are entire, this implies $f=g$.
elements.sent-2411-112: In the same way that we can think of the differential equation $2x+2yy'=0$ as describing the family of all circles centered at the origin, we can think of the equation $y=\sum_{k=0 ^\infty y^{(k) (0)x^k/k!$ as describing the family of all entire functions.}}
elements.sent-2411-113: The latter is much worse behaved than the former: no two circles centered at the origin ever intersect each other, but for all $(x_0,y_0)\in\mathbb R^2$ there exist distinct entire functions whose graphs intersect at $(x_0,y_0)$.
elements.sent-2411-114: In this sense, we can say that the real plane contains no ``critical points'' of $2x+2yy'=0$, but that every point of the real plane is a ``critical point'' of $y=\sum_{k=0 ^\infty y^{(k) (0)x^k/k!$.}}
elements.sent-2411-115: We can interpret Proposition \ref{injectivityonentires as saying that in the hyperreal plane, every point on the vertical line $x=\Omega$ is a ``non-critical point'' of the family of all entire functions, for the proposition says that no two distinct entire function graphs intersect anywhere on this vertical line.}
elements.sent-2411-116: The operation $\circ$ defined on the entire numbers by $[f]\circ[g]=[f\circ g]$ (whenever $f$ and $g$ are entire) is well-defined.
elements.sent-2411-117: Clearly with $\circ$ defined as in Corollary \ref{compositioncorollary and with $\bullet'$ defined as in Corollary \ref{primewelldefdcorollary , the entire numbers satisfy the chain rule axiom, $\forall x\forall y\,(x\circ y)'=(x'\circ y)y'$.}}

elements.section-2411-004: A variant of finite calculus using an idempotent ultrafilter on $\mathbb N$
elements.sent-2411-134: In so-called finite calculus, one considers the ``derivative'' $f(x+1)-f(x)$ of $f$, see Section 2.6 of \cite{concrete .}
elements.sent-2411-135: In this section, we will investigate a variant of this derivative, namely $\Delta f(x)={ ^*f(x+\Omega)-f(x)$ where $\Omega=[n\mapsto n]$ is the canonical hyperreal in the hyperreals constructed using an idempotent ultrafilter on $\mathbb N=\{1,2,\ldots\}$ (note that we omit $0$ from $\mathbb N$).}
elements.sent-2411-136: We will show that this finite derivative $\Delta$ has unexpected connections to the standard derivative, and that the equivalence class (in an iterated ultrapower construction) of $(\Delta f)|_\mathbb N$ is well-defined as a function of $[f|_\mathbb N]$ (we elaborate what we mean by ``in an iterated ultrapower construction'' in Remark \ref{iteratedultrafilterremark below).}

elements.sent-2411-140: An ultrafilter $q$ on $\mathbb N$ is idempotent if $q=\{S\subseteq \mathbb N\,:\, \{n\in\mathbb N\,:\,S-n\in q\}\in q\}$.
elements.sent-2411-141: Idempotent ultrafilters on $\mathbb N$ exist and are free.
elements.sent-2411-143: If $q$ is a free ultrafilter on $\mathbb N$, we write ${ ^*\mathbb R_q$ for the hyperreal numbers constructed using $q$ in the usual way, and for each $f:\mathbb N\to\mathbb R$ we write $[f]_q$ for the hyperreal represented by $f$.}
elements.sent-2411-144: If $q$ is clear from context, we will write ${ ^*\mathbb R$ for ${ ^*\mathbb R_q$, $[f]$ for $[f]_q$, and ${ ^*f$ for the nonstandard extension ${ ^*f:{ ^*\mathbb R\to{ ^*\mathbb R$ of $f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R$.}}}}}}

elements.subsection-2411-004: An alternate proof and strengthening of Hindman's theorem
elements.sent-2411-213: The well-definedness in Corollary \ref{welldefdfinitederivcorollary can be used to prove Hindman's theorem (Theorem \ref{hindmanthm below).}}
elements.sent-2411-214: Formally, our proof of Hindman's theorem is basically identical to the usual proof, but informally it appears different because all references to idempotent ultrafilters are hidden underneath the innocent-looking fact that the derivative of a constant function is zero.
elements.sent-2411-215: But the idempotency of the underlying ultrafilter was used to prove that the derivative in question is well-defined.
elements.sent-2411-217: If $f_1,\ldots,f_k:\mathbb N\to\mathbb R$ are such that each $[f_i]=c$, then there exist arbitrarily large $n\in\mathbb N$ such that the following requirement holds.

elements.sent-2411-222: The elements thereof witness the lemma.

elements.sent-2411-231: By induction, we obtain $n_1<n_2<\cdots$ with the above properties, which clearly proves the theorem.
elements.sent-2411-232: In the above proof, we proved more than was required.
elements.sent-2411-233: This leads to the following strengthening of Hindman's theorem.
elements.sent-2411-234: In the proof of Theorem \ref{hindmanthm , we proved the existence of $c\in\mathbb R$ and $n_1,n_2,\ldots\in \mathbb N$ such that (1) for each finite nonempty $X\subseteq \{n_1,n_2,\ldots\}$, $f(\sum X)=c$, and (2) for each finite $X\subseteq \{n_1,n_2,\ldots\}$, $[f_X]=c$, where $f_X:\mathbb N\to\mathbb R$ is defined by $f_X(n)=f((\sum X)+n)$.}
elements.sent-2411-235: For $X\subseteq\mathbb N$, the statement $[f_X]=c$ is equivalent to ${ ^*f_X(\Omega)=c$, which is equivalent to ${ ^*f(\sum(X\cup\{\Omega\}))=c$.}}
elements.sent-2411-236: In a heuristical sense, our proof of Theorem \ref{hindmanthm seems to suggest that the idempotency requirement might be indispensable for Corollary \ref{welldefdfinitederivcorollary : if the corollary could be proven using weaker assumptions about $q$, then we would have a non-idempotent ultrafilter proof of Hindman's theorem, which seems like it would be surprising.}}

elements.sent-2411-241: But since $\llbracket\Delta f\rrbracket$ only depends on $f|_\mathbb N$, the following lemma shows that the $\gamma$-periodic hypothesis in Theorem \ref{trickygammatheorem does not limit the $\bullet'$-like nature of the derivative from Corollary \ref{welldefdfinitederivcorollary at all.}}
elements.sent-2411-243: For every $f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R$, there exists a $\gamma$-periodic function $\hat f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R$ such that $f|_\mathbb N=\hat f|_\mathbb N$.
elements.sent-2411-244: Define $\hat f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R$ by \begin{equation}\hat f(x) =  f(n) if $x=m\gamma+n$ for some $m\in\mathbb Z, n\in\mathbb N$, 0 in any other case. \end{equation} Since $\gamma$ is irrational, there do not exist distinct ways to write $x=m\gamma+n$, thus $\hat f(x)$ is well-defined.

elements.sent-2411-265: For every $n\in\mathbb N$, \begin{equation}D_\gamma (f\circ\RM_\gamma)(n) = st(\Delta (f\circ\RM_\gamma)(n)/[\RM_\gamma]) \mbox{(Definition \ref{Dgammadefn})} = st(\Delta (f\circ\overline{\RM}_\gamma)(n)/[\RM_\gamma]) \mbox{($\RM_\gamma|_\mathbb N=\overline{\RM}_\gamma|_\mathbb N$)} = (f\circ\overline{\RM}_\gamma)'(n) \mbox{(Theorem \ref{trickygammatheorem})} = f'(\overline{\RM}_\gamma(n)) (By $*$) = f'(\RM_\gamma(n)), \mbox{($\RM_\gamma|_\mathbb N=\overline{\RM}_\gamma|_\mathbb N$)}\end{equation} so $[n\mapsto D_\gamma (f\circ\RM_\gamma)(n)]=[n\mapsto f'(\RM_\gamma(n))]$, i.e., $D_\gamma [f\circ \RM_\gamma]=[f'\circ \RM_\gamma]$.
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}D_\gamma (f\circ\RM_\gamma)(n) = st(\Delta (f\circ\RM_\gamma)(n)/[\RM_\gamma]) \mbox{(Definition \ref{Dgammadefn})} = st(\Delta (f\circ\overline{\RM}_\gamma)(n)/[\RM_\gamma]) \mbox{($\RM_\gamma|_\mathbb N=\overline{\RM}_\gamma|_\mathbb N$)} = (f\circ\overline{\RM}_\gamma)'(n) \mbox{(Theorem \ref{trickygammatheorem})} = f'(\overline{\RM}_\gamma(n)) (By $*$) = f'(\RM_\gamma(n)), \mbox{($\RM_\gamma|_\mathbb N=\overline{\RM}_\gamma|_\mathbb N$)}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-267: Thus, $D_\gamma$ follows the familiar derivative rules from elementary calculus as long as we consider functions not of the continuous variable $x\in\mathbb R$, but rather of the discrete variable $\RM_\gamma(n)\in\RM_\gamma(\mathbb N)$.
